items.title: Default to Python's built-in sort
items.avoid: Hand-rolling quicksort or mergesort in production.
items.why: Timsort is stable, adaptive, written in C, and battle-tested. A custom implementation rarely matches it in real workloads.

items.title: Use `key=` instead of decorate-sort-undecorate
items.avoid: Building `[(score, c) for c in chunks]`, sorting, then stripping.
items.why: Cleaner, no extra allocations, and the callable is computed once per element by `sorted`.

items.title: Use `heapq.nlargest`/`nsmallest` for top-k
items.why: O(n log K) vs O(n log n) — much cheaper when K ≪ n.
items.example:
```python
import heapq
top10 = heapq.nlargest(10, chunks, key=lambda c: c.score)
```

items.title: Exploit stability for multi-key sorts
items.avoid: Inventing custom comparators or tuple keys when not needed.
items.prefer: Sort by secondary key first, then primary — Timsort's stability does the rest.
items.why: Simpler code, often faster than building tuples.
items.example:
```python
rows.sort(key=lambda r: r.time)     # secondary
rows.sort(key=lambda r: -r.score)    # primary; stable wrt the previous
```

items.title: Don't re-sort on every insert
items.prefer: `bisect.insort(xs, y)` for small lists, `sortedcontainers.SortedList` for large streams.
items.why: Each full sort is O(n log n); insort is O(n) (still bad in a loop); SortedList is O(log n) per insert.
items.example:
```python
from sortedcontainers import SortedList
sl = SortedList()
for y in stream(): sl.add(y)
```
